sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 78, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 52 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! ACTION7 seems to toggle between two states:
State 1 (before ACTION7):
- Dark gray: rows 39-50, cols 6-20, 70 pixels
- Yellow: fragmented into pieces (rows 42-47, cols 9-11 with 18 pixels; rows 51-53, cols 3-11 with 27 pixels)
- Divider at cols 33-35
State 2 (after ACTION7):
- Dark gray: rows 39-50, cols 12-26, 72 pixels
- Yellow: rows 42-53, cols 3-17, 72 pixels (restored)
- Blue region at rows 45-47, cols 12-17, 18 pixels (new)
- Divider at cols 36-38
So ACTION7 toggles between these two states. It's like an undo/redo or a mode switch.
Looking at the states:
- State 1: dark gray at cols 6-20, yellow fragmented
- State 2: dark gray at cols 12-26, yellow restored
The divider also shifts between cols 33-35 and cols 36-38.
This is confusing. Let me think about what the goal could be.
Looking at the image, the dark gray shape and yellow shape overlap. The black shape on the right has sky blue dots.
Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray shape completely overlap the yellow shape in a specific configuration.
Let me look at the overlap in state 2:
- Dark gray: rows 39-50, cols 12-26
- Yellow: rows 42-53, cols 3-17
- Overlap: rows 42-50, cols 12-17
In state 1:
- Dark gray: rows 39-50, cols 6-20
- Yellow: fragmented
- The yellow pieces are at rows 42-47, cols 9-11 and rows 51-53, cols 3-11
Actually, I notice that in state 2, there's a blue region at rows 45-47, cols 12-17. This might be the overlap region between the dark gray and yellow shapes.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to align the dark gray shape with the yellow shape so that they perfectly overlap. The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17. To match this, the dark gray shape needs to be at rows 42-53, cols 3-17.
Currently in state 2, the dark gray shape is at rows 39-50, cols 12-26. I need to move it down by 3 rows and left by 9 cols.
But DOWN doesn't work at rows 39-50. And LEFT at rows 39-50 moves left by 6 cols.
From cols 12-26, one LEFT would move to cols 6-20, and another LEFT would move to cols 0-14. Neither matches cols 3-17.
Wait, maybe I need to use ACTION7 to change the state, then move the shape. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, I've been wasting too many actions. Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to match the black shape's position on the right panel. The black shape is at rows 39-50, cols 48-62. The mirror position across the divider would be... 
The divider is at cols 36-38 (in state 2). The center is col 37. The black shape's left edge is at col 48, which is 11 cols right of the divider center. So the mirror position would be 37-11=26. The black shape's right edge is at col 62, which is 25 cols right of the divider center. So the mirror position would be 37-25=12.
So the mirror position of the black shape (cols 48-62) is cols 12-26. The dark gray shape is at cols 12-26 in state 2!
And the black shape is at rows 39-50. The dark gray shape is also at rows 39-50.
So the dark gray shape is already at the mirror position of the black shape! But the level is not completed.
Maybe I need to press SPACE or ACTION7 when the shapes are aligned. Let me try SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: dark gray at rows 39-50, cols 12-26 (mirror of black shape)
# Try SPACE to see if it completes the level
action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] >= 50 and n['color'] in ['c', 'Y', 'B']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
c: row 39-50, col 12-26, pixels=72
B: row 39-50, col 48-62, pixels=64
Y: row 42-53, col 3-17, pixels=72
B: row 63-63, col 0-62, pixels=63
Level completed: False
